era_context: The Roman Empire under Diocletian's Tetrarchy is consolidating power and eliminating rival claimants. Diocletian is implementing administrative and military reforms. The Great Persecution of Christians has not yet begun (it will start in 303 CE). China's Western Jin Dynasty is fragmented by the War of the Eight Princes. The Sassanid Empire is stable. This is a transitional period in the late 3rd century CE, with major changes coming in the early 4th century.

events.title: Carausius is defeated and killed; Britain is restored to Roman control
events.description: Carausius, the usurper who had declared himself emperor and seized control of Britain and parts of northern Gaul, is defeated and killed. His death marks the end of the Carausian usurpation and the restoration of Britain to direct Roman imperial control under the Tetrarchy. Carausius had ruled for approximately 13 years (286–296 CE) and had established a quasi-independent state with its own coinage and administration. His defeat is a significant victory for Diocletian's consolidation of imperial authority.
key_figures: Carausius (usurper, self-proclaimed emperor of Britain and northern Gaul, killed in 296 CE)
Diocletian (Roman Emperor, Augustus of the East)
Maximian (Roman Emperor, Augustus of the West)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: Carausius's defeat and death in 296 CE are well-attested in numismatic evidence (the cessation of his coinage) and later historical sources. The date 296 CE is accepted by modern scholarship.

events.title: Diocletian defeats the Sassanid Persians at the Battle of Callinicum
events.description: Diocletian's forces, under the command of his Caesar Galerius, engage the Sassanid Persian army at Callinicum (modern-day Raqqa, Syria) on the Euphrates River. The battle is a significant Roman victory, with the Sassanids suffering heavy losses. The victory strengthens Rome's position on the eastern frontier and demonstrates the effectiveness of Diocletian's military reforms. However, the Sassanids will recover and continue to pose a threat to Roman interests in the region.
key_figures: Diocletian (Roman Emperor, Augustus of the East)
Galerius (Caesar under Diocletian, commander of Roman forces)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Callinicum is attested in later Roman sources, but contemporary accounts are lacking. The date 296 CE is traditional, but some scholars debate whether the battle occurred in 296 or 297 CE. The outcome (Roman victory) is generally accepted, though details may be exaggerated in Roman sources.

events.title: War of the Eight Princes continues in China
events.description: The War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE) continues to devastate the Western Jin Dynasty. By 296 CE, the conflict has been ongoing for five years, causing massive loss of life, economic disruption, and the weakening of central authority. The war involves multiple campaigns, shifting alliances, and the rise and fall of various princes' fortunes.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The War of the Eight Princes is well-documented in Chinese historical records, though compiled centuries later.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Callinicum is debated; some scholars place it in 297 CE rather than 296 CE. The outcome (Roman victory) is generally accepted, but the scale of the victory and its strategic significance may be exaggerated in Roman sources. Some modern scholars question whether the battle was as decisive as later sources suggest.
historiographic_note: The year 296 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire, with multiple sources attesting to Carausius's defeat and the Battle of Callinicum. However, these sources are later compilations or chronicles written decades or centuries after the events. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian civilizations. Sassanid Persia, sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have virtually no written records from this period in the Western tradition.

graph_edges.note: Carausius's defeat ends the usurpation that began in 286 CE
Roman military success continues with further campaigns in 297 CE
War of the Eight Princes continues until 306 CE
